work_experience.description: Alongside my PhD I consulted and freelanced as a software engineer on a number of research projects. I developed websites, built a web application for women’s rights activists, and helped create wearable technologies to help people in later life cope with death and grief. Between my undergraduate and master’s degrees I also interned at Open Lab, developing a cross-platform mobile application that encouraged conversation between younger people and people with dementia. On this project I led the application’s design and development through a co-design process with young carers of people with dementia. I was the technical lead on most of these projects, working with technologies such as Vue.js, WordPress, Node.js, TypeScript, Nginx, Docker, and MongoDB. Examples of my work can be found in the papers cited below, where I developed the technologies they centre on.

At Qred I worked as a backend software engineer, helping to maintain and develop the company's core infrastructure. I helped redesign and extend Qred's backend to meet the demands of a rapidly growing customer base, using tools such as Java, C#, Kubernetes, and CI/CD.

As a software engineer at Doctolib I specialised in Ruby on Rails and React. I spearheaded projects focused on refactoring, maintaining, and modernising critical components of the codebase. I collaborated closely with cross-functional teams, mentored fellow engineers, and led significant projects driving major product updates.

At 13|37 I consulted on varied and challenging software projects across Stockholm. I predominantly worked with JavaScript, Node.js, Express, and React to meet clients' technical needs, adopting other technologies as necessary. I worked with microservice architectures in both small and large teams, using CI/CD and Terraform to manage projects. I acted as a technical partner to clients, helping them identify, plan, and deliver on their software development needs.

At TWAICE I work in a team building software that provides insights into battery energy storage systems (BESS). I lead domain-level initiatives, such as shaping testing strategies and championing best practices around clean code and effective team processes. I also lead team-level projects involving big data, using tools such as Databricks to handle very large datasets and surface meaningful insights into battery performance. My day-to-day work spans the full stack, using FastAPI, Angular, and AWS to deliver reliable, scalable products.

education.description: During my undergraduate degree I learnt a diverse range of skills in computing science, including software design, web and app development, and human-computer interaction. I used these skills to co-design a web application with older people, helping those facing age-related conditions share healthy recipes with others who have similar dietary requirements. I also learnt the fundamentals of software development practices, algorithm design, system security, cryptography, and fault tolerance.

My master’s focused on developing technologies to aid social care. I conducted research into how technology can support intergenerational interactions between young people and people with dementia, developing a mobile application called Ticket to Talk. I worked closely with families and people with dementia in a user-centred design process, alongside care homes and local charities, to evaluate and improve the technology. The ESRC and Newcastle University ran a full press release on the project, and the UK’s Department for Business, Energy, and Industrial Strategy featured the app in their industrial campaign. Through this I developed skills in building ubiquitous technologies, peer programming, and mobile development.

I studied at Open Lab, Newcastle University, one of the largest Human Computer Interaction research groups in the world. My PhD thesis centred on supporting mental wellbeing in later life through the presentation and use of media, approached by creating technologies with a sensitive, experience-centred design method. To realise this, I created and deployed technological artefacts using technologies such as 3D printing, web applications, and mobile applications, developing a strong skillset spanning frontend and backend development, research, design, independent learning, and project management. As part of my PhD I worked with care homes in the UK and Ireland, became a member of the steering group of a local dementia charity, consulted with the Red Cross on their interaction design processes, collaborated across disciplines and institutions, and presented and published at prestigious conferences.

publications.title: Bridging the Gap: Design for Intergenerational Engagement in Dementia Care
publications.reference: Foley, S., & Welsh, D. (2020). Bridging the Gap: Design for Intergenerational Engagement in Dementia Care. In HCI and Design in the Context of Dementia (pp. 255–265). Springer, Cham. Book Chapter

(2019). ‘Blueprints’ physical to digital: curation of media to support ongoingness. In Proceedings of the Research Through Design Conference 2019 (RTD 2019).

Intersections in HCI, Design and Dementia: Inclusivity in Participatory Approaches. In Companion Publication of the 2019 on Designing Interactive Systems Conference 2019 Companion (DIS '19 Companion). ACM, New York, NY, USA, 357-360. Workshop
